mappings.rationaleAI: Global demographic shift covering characteristics of urbanization (B.1), historical urbanization (B.2), megalopolis formation (B.4.a), suburbanization (B.4.b), problems of urban growth (B.4.e), and environmental change including pollution (B.4.f).

Africa's largest city with historical development from Fatimid founding through modern expansion (B.2), facing contemporary challenges of pollution and traffic (B.4.e).
Africa's most populous urban area representing history of urbanization (B.2), emerging Abidjan-Lagos megalopolis (B.4.a), and rapid urban growth challenges (B.4.e).
China's political center for eight centuries, representing the history of urbanization (B.2) with continuous imperial and modern development.
Marxist population theory addressing the relationship between capitalism, labor, and demographic movements (A.3.e).

Second-largest global metropolitan area with medieval through modern development (B.2), facing air pollution (B.4.f) and urban sprawl challenges (B.4.e).

World's largest metropolitan economy with Dutch colonial origins (B.2), representing coalescence of metropolitan financial, cultural, and diplomatic functions (B.4.a).
Scientific study of organism-environment relationships, providing the framework for ecological theories of population-resource conservation (A.3.g).
Intensifying global interdependence driving regional economic integration between cities and hinterlands (B.4.c) and extending urban dominance through technology (B.4.d).
Island city-state representing urbanization history (B.2) and the role of technology in extending urban economic dominance (B.4.d).

Special Administrative Region as a global financial center (B.2), representing regional economic integration between city and hinterland (B.4.c).

Introduction of harmful contaminants from human activities, directly constituting problems of environmental change including pollution and climatic change (B.4.f).

Cultivation of crops and livestock relevant to ecological theories concerning population growth and natural resource conservation (A.3.g).
Plague pandemic killing 30-60% of Europe's population, representing a dramatic historical change in population through catastrophic mortality (A.2).
